papergraph.title: Recent Progress in Understanding Solid Electrolyte Interphase on Lithium Metal Anodes
papergraph.citation: Haiping Wu et al. (2020) Recent Progress in Understanding Solid Electrolyte Interphase on Lithium Metal Anodes doi:10.1002/aenm.202003092
papergraph.abstract: Lithium metal batteries (LMBs) are one of the most promising candidates for next‐generation high‐energy‐density rechargeable batteries. Solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) on Li metal anodes plays a significant role in influencing the Li deposition morphology and the cycle life of LMBs. However, a thorough understanding on the mechanisms of SEI formation and evolution is still inadequate. In this review, the progress in understanding structures, properties, and influencing factors of SEI, as well as efficient strategies of tailoring SEI are focused upon. First, the compositions, models, and recent progress in characterizing atomic structures of SEI are summarized. Second, the properties of SEI, including electronic conduction, ionic conduction, stability, and mechanical properties are elucidated. Structures and properties of SEI are greatly affected by multiple factors, thus interactions between these factors and SEI are systematically discussed. Correlations of SEI with Li deposition morphology, rate capability, and cycle life are further summarized. Moreover, efficient strategies of tailoring SEI with desired properties, including in situ SEI and ex situ SEI, are also reviewed. Finally, future directions, including in‐operando techniques, multi‐modality approaches for characterization of SEI, and artificial intelligence assisted understanding of correlations between electrolyte components and SEI properties are proposed.
papergraph.benchmark: Li deposition morphology, rate capability, cycle life
papergraph.contribution: summarized recent progress in understanding SEI structures, properties, and influencing factors, and reviewed efficient strategies of tailoring SEI

papergraph.title: Review on modeling of the anode solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) for lithium-ion batteries
papergraph.citation: Aiping Wang et al. (2018) Review on modeling of the anode solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) for lithium-ion batteries doi:10.1038/s41524-018-0064-0
papergraph.abstract: A passivation layer called the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) is formed on electrode surfaces from decomposition products of electrolytes. The SEI allows Li+ transport and blocks electrons in order to prevent further electrolyte decomposition and ensure continued electrochemical reactions. The formation and growth mechanism of the nanometer thick SEI films are yet to be completely understood owing to their complex structure and lack of reliable in situ experimental techniques. Significant advances in computational methods have made it possible to predictively model the fundamentals of SEI. This review aims to give an overview of state-of-the-art modeling progress in the investigation of SEI films on the anodes, ranging from electronic structure calculations to mesoscale modeling, covering the thermodynamics and kinetics of electrolyte reduction reactions, SEI formation, modification through electrolyte design, correlation of SEI properties with battery performance, and the artificial SEI design. Multi-scale simulations have been summarized and compared with each other as well as with experiments. Computational details of the fundamental properties of SEI, such as electron tunneling, Li-ion transport, chemical/mechanical stability of the bulk SEI and electrode/(SEI/) electrolyte interfaces have been discussed. This review shows the potential of computational approaches in the deconvolution of SEI properties and design of artificial SEI. We believe that computational modeling can be integrated with experiments to complement each other and lead to a better understanding of the complex SEI for the development of a highly efficient battery in the future.
papergraph.scenario: lithium-ion batteries | anode solid electrolyte interphase (SEI)
papergraph.contribution: overview of state-of-the-art computational modeling for investigating SEI films on anodes, including electronic structure calculations to mesoscale modeling, and discussion on the integration of computational and experimental approaches
papergraph.limitation: complex structure of SEI and lack of reliable in situ experimental techniques hinder complete understanding of SEI formation and growth mechanisms

papergraph.title: Unveiling the Mystery of LiF within Solid Electrolyte Interphase in Lithium Batteries
papergraph.citation: Zhen Li et al. (2023) Unveiling the Mystery of LiF within Solid Electrolyte Interphase in Lithium Batteries. doi:10.1002/smll.202305429
papergraph.abstract: Over the past decades, significant advances have been made in lithium-ion batteries. However, further requirement on the electrochemical performance is still a powerful motivator to improve battery technology. The solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) is considered as a key component on negative electrode, having been proven to be crucial for the performance, even in safety of batteries. Although numerous studies have focused on SEI in recent years, its specific properties, including structure and composition, remain largely unclear. Particularly, LiF, a common and important component in SEI, has sparked debates among researchers, resulting in divergent viewpoints. In this review, the recent research findings on SEI and delve into the characteristics of the LiF component is aim to consolidated. The cause of SEI formation and the evolution of SEI models is summarized. The distinctive properties of SEI generated on various negative electrodes is further discussed, the ongoing scholarly controversy surrounding the function of LiF within SEI, and the specific physicochemical properties about LiF and its synergistic effect in heterogeneous components. The objective is to facilitate better understanding of SEI and the role of the LiF component, ultimately contributing to the development of Li batteries with enhanced electrochemical performance and safety for battery communities.

papergraph.title: Modeling the Solid Electrolyte Interphase: Machine Learning as a Game Changer?
papergraph.citation: D. Diddens et al. (2022) Modeling the Solid Electrolyte Interphase: Machine Learning as a Game Changer? doi:10.1002/admi.202101734
papergraph.abstract: The solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) is a complex passivation layer that forms in situ on many battery electrodes such as lithium‐intercalated graphite or lithium metal anodes. Its essential function is to prevent the electrolyte from continuous electrochemical degradation, while simultaneously allowing ions to pass through, thus constituting an electronically insulating, but ionically conducting material. Its properties crucially affect the overall performance and aging of a battery cell. Despite decades of intense research, understanding the SEI's precise formation mechanism, structure, composition, and evolution remains a conundrum. State‐of‐the‐art computational modeling techniques are powerful tools to gain additional insights, although confronted with a trade‐off between accuracy and accessible time‐ and length scales. In this review, it is discussed how recent advances in data‐driven models, especially the development of fast and accurate surrogate simulators and deep generative models, can work with physics‐based and physics‐informed approaches to enable the next generation of breakthroughs in this field. Machine learning‐enhanced multiscale models can provide new pathways to inverse the design of interphases with desired properties.

papergraph.title: Decoupled Ion Transport via Triadic Molecular Synergy in Flame‐Retardant Quasi‐Solid Electrolytes for Safe Lithium Metal Batteries
papergraph.citation: Kun Li et al. (2025) Decoupled Ion Transport via Triadic Molecular Synergy in Flame‐Retardant Quasi‐Solid Electrolytes for Safe Lithium Metal Batteries doi:10.1002/aenm.202501236
papergraph.abstract: Ionic liquids (IL)‐based quasi‐solid polymer electrolytes (QSPEs) hold promise for safe lithium metal batteries owing to their tunable electrochemical properties and processability. However, traditional design strategy has ignored the interdependencies among “component‐function‐interface”, leading to compromised practical applications hindered by sluggish lithium‐ion transport kinetics and safety concerns. Herein, a triadic molecular synergy paradigm is proposed to decouple lithium‐ion conduction mechanisms in flame‐retardant QSPEs. Pentaerythritol tetraacrylate‐lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI) provides the structural framework, while the IL (1‐butyl‐3‐methylimidazole bis (trifluoromethylsulfonyl) imide, BmimTFSI) as a plasticizer softens the polymer chains by weakening the intermolecular forces to provide an additional ion‐transport pathway while imparting flame‐retardant properties. Additionally, the highly electronegative fluorine atoms of the additive (2‐(perfluorohexyl)ethyl methacrylate, PFMA) promote LiTFSI dissociation through electron cloud migration, simultaneously immobilizing TFSI⁻ anions and suppressing cationic competition through strong PFMA−Bmim+ coordination. As a proof‐of‐concept, this synergistic design achieves a high lithium‐ion transference number (0.72), forms a stable lithium fluoride‐dominated interphases, and enhances battery safety via a condensed‐phase flame‐retardant mechanism. Experimental validation demonstrates that the designed quasi‐solid electrolyte significantly enhances cycling stability in Li symmetric cells, Li||LiFePO4 and Li||LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cells. The proposed molecular engineering strategy establishes a paradigm for developing high‐performance QSPEs in lithium metal batteries.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.

papergraph.title: Understanding the Design Principles of Advanced Aqueous Zinc‐Ion Battery Cathodes: From Transport Kinetics to Structural Engineering, and Future Perspectives
papergraph.citation: Bo Yong et al. (2020) Understanding the Design Principles of Advanced Aqueous Zinc‐Ion Battery Cathodes: From Transport Kinetics to Structural Engineering, and Future Perspectives doi:10.1002/aenm.202002354
papergraph.abstract: Rechargeable aqueous zinc‐ion batteries (AZIBs) have attracted extensive attention and are considered to be promising energy storage devices, owing to their low cost, eco‐friendliness, and high security. However, insufficient energy density has become the bottleneck for practical applications, which is greatly influenced by their cathodes and makes the exploration of high‐performance cathodes still a great challenge. This review underscores the recent advances in the rational design of advanced cathodes for AZIBs. The review starts with a brief summary and evaluation of cathode material systems, as well as the introduction of proposed storage mechanisms. Then, fundamental problems associated with ion and electron transport behaviors inside the electrode will be pointed out and followed by potential solutions, aiming to reveal the correlation between cathode architecture design and efficient transport kinetics through structural engineering. Afterward, the structural engineering for designing advanced cathodes, including interlayer intercalation, doping effects, defect engineering, surface coatings, composite formation, and morphology control, are summarized and discussed from the view of experimental and theoretical results. Finally, the critical research challenges and future perspectives on advanced cathode materials as well as the potential developing directions of AZIBs are also given.

papergraph.title: Zinc Ion Transport Kinetics in Zinc‐based Batteries and Its Regulation Strategy
papergraph.citation: Yunting Yang et al. (2025) Zinc Ion Transport Kinetics in Zinc‐based Batteries and Its Regulation Strategy doi:10.1002/aenm.202500316
papergraph.abstract: Rechargeable zinc‐ion batteries (ZIBs) have gained significant attention as potential next‐generation energy storage systems, owing to their inherent safety, environmental benignity, and cost‐effectiveness. However, the substantial electrostatic repulsion of Zn ion results in a sluggish kinetics for its insertion into the cathode material. Meanwhile, the formation of hydrated ionic groups with increased mass and volume in the aqueous electrolyte further hampers the transport ability of zinc ions, significantly impacting the overall electrochemical performance (including capacity, energy density, rate‐capability, and cyclability) of aqueous zinc‐ion batteries. This review systematically summarized the recent progress in the regulation strategy of the zinc‐ion transport kinetics. The as‐reported mechanisms are introduced for zinc ion transport in ZIBs (Zn2+ insertion/extraction mechanism, H+ or H2O/ Zn2+ co‐insertion/extraction mechanism, conversion reaction mechanism, and coordination reaction mechanism). Then, cathode material design for fast zinc‐ion transport kinetics including soft lattice construction, doping effects, defect introduction, morphology control, and interface design is systematically summarized. Finally, it is concluded with future research directions, such as high‐entropy design, multi‐scale simulation, and machine study, providing a roadmap for developing high‐performance zinc ion batteries at ultralow operation temperatures.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.

papergraph.benchmark: capacity, energy density, rate-capability, and cyclability

papergraph.title: Recent advances in rational design for high-performance potassium-ion batteries
papergraph.citation: Yifan Xu et al. (2024) Recent advances in rational design for high-performance potassium-ion batteries. doi:10.1039/d3cs00601h
papergraph.abstract: The growing global energy demand necessitates the development of renewable energy solutions to mitigate greenhouse gas emissions and air pollution. To efficiently utilize renewable yet intermittent energy sources such as solar and wind power, there is a critical need for large-scale energy storage systems (EES) with high electrochemical performance. While lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been successfully used for EES, the surging demand and price, coupled with limited supply of crucial metals like lithium and cobalt, raised concerns about future sustainability. In this context, potassium-ion batteries (PIBs) have emerged as promising alternatives to commercial LIBs. Leveraging the low cost of potassium resources, abundant natural reserves, and the similar chemical properties of lithium and potassium, PIBs exhibit excellent potassium ion transport kinetics in electrolytes. This review starts from the fundamental principles and structural regulation of PIBs, offering a comprehensive overview of their current research status. It covers cathode materials, anode materials, electrolytes, binders, and separators, combining insights from full battery performance, degradation mechanisms, in situ/ex situ characterization, and theoretical calculations. We anticipate that this review will inspire greater interest in the development of high-efficiency PIBs and pave the way for their future commercial applications.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.
papergraph.contribution: comprehensive overview of the current research status of potassium-ion batteries, covering cathode materials, anode materials, electrolytes, binders, and separators
papergraph.limitation: Leveraging the low cost of potassium resources, abundant natural reserves, and the similar chemical properties of lithium and potassium, PIBs exhibit excellent potassium ion transport kinetics in electrolytes.

papergraph.title: Electrolyte Engineering Enables High Performance Zinc-Ion Batteries
papergraph.citation: Yanyan Wang et al. (2022) Electrolyte Engineering Enables High Performance Zinc-Ion Batteries. doi:10.1002/smll.202107033
papergraph.abstract: Zinc-ion batteries (ZIBs) feature high safety, low cost, environmental-friendliness, and promising electrochemical performance, and are therefore regarded as a potential technology to be applied in large-scale energy storage devices. However, ZIBs still face some critical challenges and bottlenecks. The electrolyte is an essential component of batteries and its properties affect the mass transport, energy storage mechanisms, reaction kinetics, and side reactions of ZIBs. The adjustment of electrolyte formulas usually has direct and obvious impacts on the overall output and performance. In this review, advanced electrolyte strategies are overviewed for optimizing the compatibility between cathode materials and electrolytes, inhibiting anode corrosion and dendrite growth, extending electrochemical stability windows, enabling wearable applications, and enhancing temperature tolerance. The underlying scientific mechanisms, electrolyte design principles, and recent progress are presented to provide a better understanding and inspiration to readers. In addition, a comprehensive perspective about electrolyte design and engineering for ZIBs is included.

papergraph.title: Interphases in Lithium–Sulfur Batteries: Toward Deployable Devices with Competitive Energy Density and Stability
papergraph.citation: Qing Zhao et al. (2018) Interphases in Lithium–Sulfur Batteries: Toward Deployable Devices with Competitive Energy Density and Stability doi:10.1021/ACSENERGYLETT.8B01001
papergraph.abstract: Lithium sulfur batteries have been studied intensively because of their potential to create high specific energies that meet accepted targets for mobile and stationary storage. Substantial efforts have been devoted to improving the electron/ion conductivity of the sulfur cathode, suppressing dissolution of polysulfide, and limiting parasitic reactions of polysulfide with lithium. Less consideration has addressed the interface stability of lithium, which is critical for long-term stability of cells that utilize high sulfur loadings and minimal electrolyte volume to maximize overall specific energy. Recent emphasis on all-solid-state batteries underscores the important role well-formed interphases must play in achieving high material utilization at practical rates. We suggest that advances toward practical Li–S cells will come from strategies for creating durable interphases on lithium that limit active material and electrolyte loss and that provide greater flexibility in electrolyte design.
improvements: No explicit limitation extracted.
papergraph.contribution: suggesting the importance of creating durable interphases on lithium for long-term stability and high material utilization in lithium-sulfur batteries
papergraph.limitation: Full text excerpt: suggesting the importance of creating durable interphases on lithium for long-term stability and high material utilization in lithium-sulfur batteries No explicit limitation extracted.

keynotes.title: Layered Oxide Cathodes for Sodium-Ion Batteries: Storage Mechanism, Electrochemistry, and Techno-economics

keynote.title: Layered Oxide Cathodes for Sodium-Ion Batteries: Storage Mechanism, Electrochemistry, and Techno-economics
keynote.core_problem: The development of cost-effective, high-performance, and stable cathode materials for sodium-ion batteries (NIBs) to serve as an alternative to lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), especially for large-scale energy storage systems.
contributions: Techno-economic analysis of NaxTMO2 cathodes for NIBs
Investigation into the degradation mechanism of NaxTMO2 upon cycling and air exposure
Development of strategies to improve electrochemical performance and air stability of NaxTMO2
Discussion on the structure-performance relationships and general design strategies for NaxTMO2
limitations: Irreversible phase transformations during cycling
Poor air stability
Complex charge-compensation mechanisms
Relatively high cost of the full cell compared to LiFePO4-based LIBs

gap_signals.text: Complex charge-compensation mechanisms
Irreversible phase transformations during cycling
Poor air stability
Relatively high cost of the full cell compared to LiFePO4-based LIBs
Improving the stability of NaxTMO2 toward ambient air and upon cycling
Reducing the cost and enhancing the large-scale fabrication capability of NaxTMO2

important_claims.claim: Sodium resources are abundantly distributed in the earth’s crust, making NIBs a viable alternative for large-scale energy storage.
important_claims.evidence: First, sodium resources are abundantly distributed in the earth’s crust. Second, high-performance NIB cathode materials can be fabricated by using solely inexpensive and noncritical transition metals such as manganese and iron, which further reduces the cost of the required raw materials.

important_claims.claim: NaxTMO2 cathodes have the potential to reduce costs, increase energy density and cycling stability, and improve the safety of NIBs for large-scale energy storage.
important_claims.evidence: Sodium layered transition metal oxides (NaxTMO2, TM = transition metal/s), such as Mn-based sodium layered oxides, represent an important family of cathode materials with the potential to reduce costs, increase energy density and cycling stability, and improve the safety of NIBs for large-scale energy storage.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future directions for the development of NaxTMO2 cathodes for NIBs, highlighting key challenges and opportunities that can guide future research and development efforts.

keynotes.title: Lithium-Ion Battery Recycling in the Circular Economy: A Review

keynote.title: Lithium-Ion Battery Recycling in the Circular Economy: A Review
keynote.core_problem: The need for effective recycling and recovery of materials from spent lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) to support a circular economy, with limited attention to policy and regulatory affairs.
contributions: Reviewed 93 articles on LIB recycling and the circular economy, identifying key trends and gaps in the research.
Highlighted the importance of pre-treatment processes in hydrometallurgy and direct physical recycling (DPR).
Provided future research directions for academics and policymakers.
methods: Literature review of 93 articles (66 original research articles and 27 review articles) from the Web of Science core collection database.
experiments_or_evidence: Analysis of publication trends showing an exponential increase in studies focusing on recycling and recovery-related issues.
limitations: Policy and regulatory affairs received less attention than recycling in the reviewed literature.

gap_signals.text: Policy and regulatory affairs received less attention than recycling in the reviewed literature.
Application of the circular business model, stakeholder engagement, clear policy guidelines, extended producer responsibility, and material tracking and identification require further focus.
Further consideration is needed for techno-economic assessment of the recycling process, safe reverse logistics, and lifecycle assessment of both hydrometallurgical and pyrometallurgical processes.

datasets_or_materials: Web of Science core collection database

important_claims.claim: Publications in the area of LIB recycling and the circular economy are increasing exponentially.
important_claims.evidence: The study analyzed 93 articles, noting a significant rise in research focused on recycling and recovery-related issues.

important_claims.claim: Direct physical recycling (DPR) is a promising recycling technique that requires further attention.
important_claims.evidence: Pre-treatment processes, which are critical for DPR, were widely discussed in the reviewed literature.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive overview of current research trends and identifies key areas for future investigation, such as the techno-economic assessment of recycling processes, stakeholder engagement, and the development of circular business models. It serves as a valuable resource for researchers and policymakers looking to advance the field of LIB recycling within the circular economy.

keynotes.title: A Perspective on Pathways Toward Commercial Sodium‐Ion Batteries

keynote.title: A Perspective on Pathways Toward Commercial Sodium‐Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: The commercialization of sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) faces significant challenges, particularly in grid storage and automotive applications, despite their potential as a cost-effective and abundant alternative to lithium-ion batteries (LIBs).
contributions: Identifies critical challenges in making SIBs viable from both chemical and techno-economic perspectives.
Provides a comparison of materials chemistry, working mechanisms, and cost between mainstream LIB systems and prospective SIB systems.

gap_signals.text: The intrinsic challenges of SIBs regarding storage stability, capacity utilization, cycle stability, calendar life, and safe operation of cathode, electrolyte, and anode materials are discussed, indicating areas that need further research.

Issues related to the scalability of material production, materials engineering feasibility, and energy-dense electrode design and fabrication are highlighted, suggesting technological advancements are needed.

important_claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are emerging as a promising alternative to LIBs due to the high abundance and low cost of sodium and other raw materials.
important_claims.evidence: The article discusses the advantages of SIBs in terms of resource availability and cost, which are key factors for large-scale adoption.

important_claims.claim: Achieving high-energy-density, stable, and cost-effective SIBs is crucial for their commercialization.
important_claims.evidence: The perspective lists and discusses promising pathways toward these goals, emphasizing the need for addressing current challenges.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the challenges and potential solutions for commercializing SIBs, which can guide future research and development efforts in this field.

keynotes.title: Comprehensive Review of Emerging Lithium and Sodium-Ion Electrochemical Systems for Advanced Energy Storage Applications

keynote.title: Comprehensive Review of Emerging Lithium and Sodium-Ion Electrochemical Systems for Advanced Energy Storage Applications
keynote.core_problem: The need for effective, scalable, and sustainable energy storage solutions due to the rapid growth of electric vehicles, portable devices, and renewable energy sources, while addressing the limitations of lithium resources.
contributions: Thorough analysis of Li-ion and Na-ion electrochemical systems, including fundamental concepts, recent developments, and new challenges.
Comparison of energy density, rate capability, cycle stability, environmental impact, and cost-efficiency between Li-ion and Na-ion systems.
Discussion on the commercialization and scaling prospects of next-generation batteries, with a focus on recycling, green synthesis, and smart grid integration.
Evaluation of the environmental and techno-economic effects of transitioning from lithium to sodium-based chemistry.

gap_signals.text: Identifying technological gaps in current Li-ion and Na-ion battery technologies.
Outlining future research goals that could advance the development of sustainable, high-performance electrochemical energy storage systems.

important_claims.claim: Li-ion batteries have dominated the energy storage market due to their long cycle life, high energy density, and established manufacturing infrastructure.
important_claims.evidence: Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries have long dominated the energy storage market. However, the search for alternative technologies, especially sodium-ion (Na-ion) batteries, has accelerated due to the limited supply and increasing cost of lithium resources.

important_claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries are being considered as an alternative due to the abundance and lower cost of sodium compared to lithium.
important_claims.evidence: The hunt for substitute technologies, especially sodium-ion (Na-ion) batteries, which take advantage of the cheap and plentiful sodium, has accelerated due to the limited supply and growing expense of lithium resources.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive summary of existing knowledge, identifies technological gaps, and outlines future research directions, which can guide the development of sustainable, high-performance electrochemical energy storage systems.

keynotes.title: Development of High-Performance Manganese-Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes for Lithium-Ion Batteries

keynote.title: Development of High-Performance Manganese-Rich Layered Oxide Cathodes for Lithium-Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: The need for high-performance, resource-abundant cathode materials for sustainable energy storage systems, particularly for electric vehicles (EVs) and grid-scale applications.
contributions: Design and electrochemical evaluation of Mn-rich layered oxide compositions
Achievement of stable cycling performance in full-cell configurations through systematic tuning of transition metal content and lithium excess
Demonstration of high-capacity retention at practical loadings and voltages
Techno-economic analysis supporting the commercial potential of Mn-based layered oxides
methods: Systematic tuning of transition metal content and lithium excess
Electrochemical evaluation of Mn-rich layered oxide compositions
experiments_or_evidence: Stable cycling performance in full-cell configurations
High-capacity retention at practical loadings and voltages

gap_signals.text: Further research is needed to fully understand the long-term stability and degradation mechanisms of these Mn-rich layered oxides.

important_claims.claim: Mn-rich layered oxides deliver high-capacity retention at practical loadings and voltages, making them suitable for EVs and grid-scale applications.
important_claims.evidence: Electrochemical evaluations show stable cycling performance in full-cell configurations.

important_claims.claim: Techno-economic analysis supports the commercial potential of Mn-based layered oxides by projecting competitiveness with commercial cathode materials.
important_claims.evidence: Analysis indicates that these materials are economically viable compared to existing commercial cathode materials.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The findings and methods can be used as a foundation for further research into the development and optimization of manganese-rich layered oxides for lithium-ion batteries, particularly in enhancing their performance and commercial viability.

keynotes.title: Second-life battery systems for affordable energy access in Kenyan primary schools

keynote.title: Second-life battery systems for affordable energy access in Kenyan primary schools
keynote.core_problem: The growing demand for energy storage is leading to constraints on raw battery materials, reducing the availability of new batteries, and increasing battery waste. There is a need to explore the reuse and repurposing of retired (second-life) batteries to provide affordable energy access, particularly in contexts like primary schools in Kenya.
contributions: Exploration of the feasibility of using second-life batteries and solar photovoltaics for affordable energy access in Kenyan primary schools.
Comparison of the cost and performance of systems using second-life batteries versus new batteries.
methods: Interviews with 12 East African schools to determine realistic system sizes.
Simulation of 48 different system scenarios with varying solar photovoltaic sizes and lithium-ion battery capacities.
Sensitivity analysis of battery transportation costs (local vs. imported).
Techno-economic analysis to compare the levelized cost of electricity and payback periods of systems using second-life batteries versus new batteries.
experiments_or_evidence: Simulations of 48 system scenarios with varying solar photovoltaic sizes (5–10 kW in 2.5 kW increments) and lithium-ion battery capacities (5–20 kWh in 5 kWh increments).
Sensitivity analysis of battery transportation costs (local vs. imported).
assumptions: Retired batteries are difficult to recycle into components.
Second-life batteries can be effectively repurposed for use in primary schools.
limitations: The study focuses specifically on primary schools in Kenya, which may not be generalizable to other contexts or regions.
The simulations and analyses are based on a limited set of scenarios and may not cover all possible variations.

gap_signals.text: The simulations and analyses are based on a limited set of scenarios and may not cover all possible variations.
The study focuses specifically on primary schools in Kenya, which may not be generalizable to other contexts or regions.
Further studies are needed to explore the long-term performance and reliability of second-life battery systems in various educational and community settings.
Policies and incentives that support the use of second-life batteries could be beneficial for promoting sustainable and affordable energy access.

datasets_or_materials: Data from interviews with 12 East African schools.
code_or_implementation: Simulation models and techno-economic analysis tools used to evaluate the scenarios.

important_claims.claim: Second-life batteries decrease the levelized cost of electricity by 5.6–35.3% in 97.2% of scenarios compared to similar systems with new batteries, and by 41.9–64.5% compared to the cost of the same energy service provided by the utility grid.
important_claims.evidence: Results from the techno-economic analysis of 48 system scenarios.

important_claims.claim: The systems with the smallest levelized cost of electricity (i.e., 0.11 USD/kWh) use either 7.5 kW or 10 kW of solar with 20 kWh of storage.
important_claims.evidence: Simulation results showing the optimal system configurations.

important_claims.claim: Across all cases, the payback period is decreased by 8.2–42.9% using second-life batteries compared to new batteries; the system with the smallest payback period (i.e., 2.9 years) uses 5 kW solar and 5 kWh storage.
important_claims.evidence: Analysis of the payback periods for the 48 system scenarios.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This research provides a framework for evaluating the feasibility and cost-effectiveness of using second-life batteries in combination with solar photovoltaics for energy access in underserved communities. The methods and findings can be adapted and applied to other contexts and regions, contributing to the broader goal of sustainable and affordable energy solutions.

keynotes.title: Unravelling the Mechanism of Pulse Current Charging for Enhancing the Stability of Commercial LiNi0.5Mn0.3Co0.2O2/Graphite Lithium‐Ion Batteries

keynote.title: Unravelling the Mechanism of Pulse Current Charging for Enhancing the Stability of Commercial LiNi0.5Mn0.3Co0.2O2/Graphite Lithium‐Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Improving the cycle stability and understanding the dynamic electrochemical processes in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) during operation, particularly with NMC532/graphite LIBs.
contributions: Demonstrated that pulse current (PC) charging enhances the cycle stability of NMC532/graphite LIBs.
Provided mechanistic insights into how PC charging affects Li-ion storage in graphite and NMC532.
methods: Electrochemical diagnosis
Operando and ex situ Raman spectroscopy
X-ray absorption spectroscopy
experiments_or_evidence: Pulse current (PC) charging mitigates the rise of battery impedance and minimizes the loss of electrode materials.
Li-ions are more uniformly intercalated into graphite under PC charging.
Ni element in NMC532 achieves a higher energy state with less Ni-O bond length variation under PC charging.
PC charging suppresses electrolyte decomposition and continuous thickening of the SEI layer on the graphite anode.

gap_signals.text: The role of PC charging in enhancing battery cycling stability provides valuable insights for future LIBs and beyond.

important_claims.claim: Pulse current (PC) charging enhances the cycle stability of NMC532/graphite LIBs.
important_claims.evidence: Electrochemical diagnosis and spectroscopic techniques show that PC charging mitigates battery impedance, minimizes loss of electrode materials, and improves Li-ion intercalation uniformity.

important_claims.claim: PC charging stabilizes Ni-O bond lengths and reduces electrolyte decomposition and SEI layer thickening.
important_claims.evidence: X-ray absorption spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy reveal that PC charging results in a higher energy state for Ni with less Ni-O bond length variation and reduced electrolyte decomposition.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The findings offer detailed mechanistic insights into the benefits of PC charging, which can be used to develop more efficient and stable cycling protocols for LIBs, potentially extending their lifespan and performance.

keynotes.title: Decoding Electrochemical Processes of Lithium‐Ion Batteries by Classical Molecular Dynamics Simulations

keynote.title: Decoding Electrochemical Processes of Lithium‐Ion Batteries by Classical Molecular Dynamics Simulations
keynote.core_problem: Understanding the electrochemistry of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) at the molecular scale to improve their performance, stability, lifetime, and safety.
contributions: Discusses developments in MD approaches using non-reactive force fields, reactive force fields, and machine learning potential for modeling chemical reactions and transport in LIBs.
Comprehensively discusses how molecular interactions, structures, transport, and reaction processes affect electrode stability, energy capacity, and interfacial properties.
methods: Classical molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with non-reactive force fields, reactive force fields, and machine learning potentials

gap_signals.text: The remaining challenges and envisioned future routes are commented on for high-fidelity, effective simulation methods to decode the invisible interactions and chemical reactions in LIBs.

important_claims.claim: MD simulations provide dynamic insights into the electrochemical processes and ion transport in LIBs during charging and discharging that are usually challenging to observe experimentally.
important_claims.evidence: This is implied as a key benefit of using MD simulations in the context of improving battery performance and understanding.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The review provides a comprehensive overview of MD simulation techniques and their application in understanding the electrochemistry of LIBs, which can guide researchers in selecting appropriate simulation methods and interpreting results for further advancements in battery technology.

keynotes.title: Natural Minerals Derived Advanced Materials for High Performance Lithium‐Ion and Sodium‐Ion Storage Systems

keynote.title: Natural Minerals Derived Advanced Materials for High Performance Lithium‐Ion and Sodium‐Ion Storage Systems
keynote.core_problem: The need for high-performance, sustainable, and cost-effective electrode materials for lithium-ion and sodium-ion storage systems, as traditional materials are expensive and have lengthy production processes.
contributions: Highlighting the potential of natural minerals as alternative, cost-effective, and sustainable electrode materials for energy storage.
Reviewing various modification strategies to enhance the electrochemical performance of natural minerals.
methods: Structural design
Surface modifications
Heteroatom doping
Heterostructure design
assumptions: Natural minerals can be effectively used as electrode materials after appropriate purification and modification.
limitations: Further research is needed to optimize the synthesis and modification of natural minerals for large-scale application.

gap_signals.text: Further research is needed to optimize the synthesis and modification of natural minerals for large-scale application.
This review calls for further research into optimizing the synthesis and modification of natural minerals, enabling their large‐scale application in energy storage technologies.

important_claims.claim: Natural minerals offer significant advantages including abundant availability, low cost, high tap density, and inherent structural diversity.
important_claims.evidence: By utilizing naturally abundant minerals, electrode material costs can be drastically reduced, especially after flotation-based purification, as well as higher tap densities can be achieved, which improves the material's application potential.

important_claims.claim: Modification strategies such as structural design, surface modifications, heteroatom doping, and heterostructure design can improve ion diffusion, conductivity, and structural stability.
important_claims.evidence: These enhancements contribute to superior energy density, power density, and cycle life in batteries.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive overview of the use of natural minerals as electrode materials, highlighting key modification strategies and calling for further research, which can guide future studies and applications in the field of energy storage.

keynotes.title: Lithiated Prussian blue analogues as positive electrode active materials for stable non-aqueous lithium-ion batteries

keynote.title: Lithiated Prussian blue analogues as positive electrode active materials for stable non-aqueous lithium-ion batteries
keynote.core_problem: Prussian blue analogues (PBAs) are not generally suitable for non-aqueous Li-ion storage due to their instability upon prolonged cycling.
contributions: Assessment of the feasibility of PBAs with various lithium content for non-aqueous Li-ion storage
Determination of the crystal structure of lithiated PBAs via neutron powder diffraction measurements
Investigation of the influence of water on structural stability and Li-ion migration through operando X-ray diffraction measurements and bond valence simulations

Demonstration of a positive electrode containing Li2-xFeFe(CN)6⋅nH2O active material in a coin cell configuration, delivering an initial discharge capacity of 142 mAh g−1 at 19 mA g−1 and a discharge capacity retention of 80.7% after 1000 cycles at 1.9 A g−1

Report of a coin cell using a graphite-based negative electrode, capable of cycling for more than 370 cycles at 190 mA g−1 with a stable discharge capacity of about 105 mAh g−1 and a discharge capacity retention of 98% at 25 °C
methods: Neutron powder diffraction measurements
Operando X-ray diffraction measurements
Bond valence simulations
experiments_or_evidence: Crystal structure determination of lithiated PBAs
Investigation of the effect of water on structural stability and Li-ion migration
Performance testing of coin cells with Li2-xFeFe(CN)6⋅nH2O as the positive electrode active material

gap_signals.text: Further enhancement of the cycle life and discharge capacity of PBAs in non-aqueous Li-ion batteries could be explored.

important_claims.claim: A positive electrode containing Li2-xFeFe(CN)6⋅nH2O delivers an initial discharge capacity of 142 mAh g−1 at 19 mA g−1 and a discharge capacity retention of 80.7% after 1000 cycles at 1.9 A g−1.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental results from coin cell tests

important_claims.claim: A coin cell with a graphite-based negative electrode can cycle for more than 370 cycles at 190 mA g−1 with a stable discharge capacity of about 105 mAh g−1 and a discharge capacity retention of 98% at 25 °C.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental results from coin cell tests

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The findings provide a foundation for developing more stable and efficient non-aqueous Li-ion batteries using lithiated Prussian blue analogues, which can guide further research into improving their performance and durability.

keynotes.title: Designing High‐Performance Dual‐Ion Batteries: Insights into Electrode, Electrolyte, and Interface Engineering

keynote.title: Designing High‐Performance Dual‐Ion Batteries: Insights into Electrode, Electrolyte, and Interface Engineering
keynote.core_problem: The energy density of dual-ion batteries (DIBs) is insufficient for commercial viability, despite their high operating voltage, low cost, and environmental sustainability.
contributions: Provides an overview of strategies to enhance carrier migration dynamics in DIBs
Discusses advancements in electrode and electrolyte material composition and design
Explores carrier transport mechanism optimization and interface engineering

gap_signals.text: Energy density remains insufficient for commercial viability

important_claims.claim: Substantial progress has been made in the innovation and modification of key materials for DIBs.
important_claims.evidence: Driven by the evolving demands of advanced energy markets and material-specific application requirements.

important_claims.claim: Critical electrochemical performance metrics are heavily influenced by the structure and properties of both electrode and electrolyte materials.
important_claims.evidence: Enhancements in these areas can improve energy density, power density, rate capability, and cycling stability.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The review provides valuable insights and strategies for enhancing the performance of DIBs, which can be used to guide future research and development efforts in this field.

keynotes.title: Achievements, challenges, and perspectives in the design of polymer binders for advanced lithium-ion batteries.

keynote.title: Achievements, challenges, and perspectives in the design of polymer binders for advanced lithium-ion batteries.
keynote.core_problem: The development of high-capacity electrode materials for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is hindered by issues such as poor adhesion, insufficient mechanical properties, and low electronic and ionic conductivity of current binder materials, particularly PVDF.
contributions: Review of recent progress in developing different polymeric materials as binders for anodes and cathodes in LIBs.
Analysis of the failure mechanisms of binders using two models: 'interface failure' and 'degradation failure'.
Proposal of several binder parameters applicable to most lithium-ion batteries.
Discussion of the relationships between the chemical structure and properties of the binder at the molecular level.
Design principles of the binder from a molecular structure point of view, with silicon and sulfur active electrode materials as examples.
methods: Literature review and analysis of existing research on polymer binders for LIBs.
Systematic consideration and summarization of the relationships between the chemical structure and properties of the binder at the molecular level.
assumptions: Polymer binders are crucial for maintaining the structural integrity and stability of electrodes in LIBs.
Improvement in the properties of polymer binders can enhance the performance of LIBs.

gap_signals.text: Further design of novel efficient binders for lithium-ion batteries, especially for high energy density electrode materials, is needed.

important_claims.claim: Polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) has superior electrochemical stability but suffers from poor adhesion, insufficient mechanical properties, and low electronic and ionic conductivity.
important_claims.evidence: The text mentions that while PVDF is a commercial binder with superior electrochemical stability, its other properties hinder its application as a high-capacity electrode material.

important_claims.claim: Different polymeric materials, including natural polymers and synthetic non-conductive and electronically conductive polymers, have been developed as binders for LIBs.
important_claims.evidence: The review highlights the recent progress in developing various polymeric materials as binders for both anodes and cathodes in LIBs.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive overview and critical analysis of the current state of polymer binders for LIBs, which can guide future research in designing more effective binders, particularly for high-energy-density applications.

keynotes.title: Amorphous Materials for Lithium-Ion and Post-Lithium-Ion Batteries

keynote.title: Amorphous Materials for Lithium-Ion and Post-Lithium-Ion Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Improving the performance of lithium-ion and post-lithium-ion batteries by utilizing amorphous materials, which can enhance ion storage, diffusion, and reaction activity, as well as tolerate volume changes.
contributions: Highlighting recent advances in using amorphous materials (AMs) for fabricating lithium-ion and post-lithium-ion batteries.
Exploring the correlation between material structure and properties (electrochemical, mechanical, chemical, and thermal).
Reviewing both conventional and emerging characterization methods for analyzing AMs.
Discussing the roles of disorder in influencing the performances of various batteries based on different metals (lithium, sodium, potassium, zinc).

gap_signals.text: The challenges and perspectives for commercializing rechargeable AMs-based batteries.

important_claims.claim: Amorphous materials are emerging as important alternatives to crystalline materials in battery technology because they can increase the number of ion storage sites and diffusion channels, enhance solid-state ion diffusion, tolerate more severe volume changes, and improve reaction activity.
important_claims.evidence: The review highlights these advantages and discusses the use of AMs in various types of batteries.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future directions of amorphous materials in battery technology, which can guide further research and development in this field.

keynotes.title: Beyond lithium-ion: emerging frontiers in next-generation battery technologies

keynote.title: Beyond lithium-ion: emerging frontiers in next-generation battery technologies
keynote.core_problem: The limitations of conventional lithium-ion batteries and the need for advanced energy storage solutions.
contributions: Detailed exploration of the latest developments and future directions in energy storage, particularly focusing on alternatives to traditional lithium-ion batteries.
Discussion of the environmental and economic implications of next-generation battery technologies.

gap_signals.text: The article identifies underlying challenges and discusses the significant impact of these new frontiers on various applications–from portable electronics to electric vehicles and grid-scale energy storage.

important_claims.claim: Solid-state batteries, lithium-sulfur systems, and other metal-ion (sodium, potassium, magnesium, and calcium) batteries are promising alternatives to traditional lithium-ion batteries.
important_claims.evidence: The article examines recent breakthroughs and the transformative potential of these next-generation battery technologies.

important_claims.claim: Next-generation battery technologies have significant implications for various applications including portable electronics, electric vehicles, and grid-scale energy storage.
important_claims.evidence: The article discusses the impact of these new frontiers on different applications, highlighting their transformative potential.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The article provides a comprehensive overview of emerging battery technologies and their potential impacts, which can be valuable for researchers looking to explore or compare different battery chemistries and their applications. It also highlights key areas for further research and development.

keynotes.title: Prospective Competence of Magnesium-ion over Lithium-ion Batteries in Electric Propulsion

keynote.title: Prospective Competence of Magnesium-ion over Lithium-ion Batteries in Electric Propulsion
keynote.core_problem: The need for sustainable and environmentally friendly battery technologies for electric vehicles (EVs) due to concerns with lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) such as resource scarcity, safety hazards, and environmental impact.
contributions: Comprehensive comparison of LIBs and MIBs based on various parameters including electrochemical performance, safety, ion transport, cycle life, and environmental impact.
methods: Review and analysis of recent developments in MIB component materials, such as bismuth-based and titanium-based anodes, transition metal oxide and chalcogenide cathodes, and hybrid or solid-state electrolytes.
assumptions: MIBs are a viable alternative to LIBs and can offer significant advantages in terms of sustainability and safety.
limitations: MIBs remain at a lower technology readiness level compared to LIBs.

gap_signals.text: MIBs remain at a lower technology readiness level compared to LIBs.
MIBs remain at a lower technology readiness level

datasets_or_materials: Bismuth-based and titanium-based anodes, transition metal oxide and chalcogenide cathodes, and hybrid or solid-state electrolytes.

important_claims.claim: MIBs can reduce the net CO2 footprint nearly to 24,800 kg when compared to 26,500 kg and 37,500 kg associated respectively with LIB powered EVs and ICEVs for a span of 10 years.
important_claims.evidence: As electrification becoming central to all powertrain architectures, the analysis highlights that although MIBs remain at a lower technology readiness level, their favorable energy densities, extended cycling stability, and reduced ecological footprint position them as strong candidate for future EV battery applications.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a detailed comparison between LIBs and MIBs, which can be used as a reference for further research and development in battery technologies, particularly focusing on improving the technological readiness of MIBs.

keynotes.title: Battery Blind Spots: A Critical Examination of Lithium-Ion Sustainability in Electric Vehicles and Pathways to Greener Alternatives

keynote.title: Battery Blind Spots: A Critical Examination of Lithium-Ion Sustainability in Electric Vehicles and Pathways to Greener Alternatives
keynote.core_problem: The widespread claim that lithium-ion batteries are a straightforward 'green' solution for electric vehicles, despite significant environmental, social, and economic trade-offs.
contributions: Critical examination of the sustainability of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles
Highlighting the need for rapid scaling of battery recycling and a shift to alternative chemistries
Providing concrete policy recommendations and innovation pathways for more sustainable and equitable electric vehicle systems
methods: Thematic analysis of real-world case studies in cities that have embraced electric mobility
Drawing from the latest peer-reviewed studies and reports published between 2024 and 2025
experiments_or_evidence: Case studies in cities with electric mobility initiatives
Peer-reviewed studies and reports on lithium-ion batteries and their alternatives

gap_signals.text: Looming economic evidence reveals significant trade-offs
Significant trade-offs in environmental damage from mining and supply chain issues
Serious social concerns in supply chains

important_claims.claim: Without rapid scaling of battery recycling and a faster shift to alternative chemistries, projected shortages of key minerals by 2030 will create severe bottlenecks.
important_claims.evidence: Analysis of the full picture of environmental damage, social concerns, and economic evidence

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This work provides a critical perspective on the sustainability of lithium-ion batteries and highlights the need for alternative chemistries and better recycling practices. It also offers concrete policy recommendations and innovation pathways, making it valuable for researchers and policymakers working on sustainable and equitable electric vehicle systems.

keynotes.title: Innovative Electrolytes Based on Ionic Liquids and Polymers for Next-Generation Solid-State Batteries

keynote.title: Innovative Electrolytes Based on Ionic Liquids and Polymers for Next-Generation Solid-State Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Current electrolytes in Li-ion batteries are not compatible with next-generation energy storage technologies, particularly those based on Li metal and other alkali metals like Na, Mg, Zn, and Al. There is a need to develop solid-state electrolytes, ionic liquids, polymers, and their combinations that can support these new battery technologies.
contributions: Development of single-ion conductors by tethering anions to the polymer backbone to increase the conductivity of lithium or sodium cations.
Use of copolymer approaches and incorporation of bulky quaternary ammonium co-cations to enhance charge separation and conductivity.
Demonstration of ion gels or composite polymer electrolytes using polymerized ionic liquid matrices for improved mechanical robustness and ionic conductivity.
methods: Experimental development and testing of new electrolyte materials
Computer simulations to model and predict performance of electrolytes
experiments_or_evidence: Experimental demonstration of enhanced conductivity in single-ion conductor systems
Computer simulation results supporting the effectiveness of copolymer and quaternary ammonium co-cation approaches
assumptions: The assumption that designing and tethering more weakly associating anions to the polymer backbone will further enhance ion transport.
limitations: Low conductivity in initial single-ion conductor designs
The challenge of decoupling ion transport from mechanical properties in ideal electrolyte materials

gap_signals.text: Low conductivity in initial single-ion conductor designs
The challenge of decoupling ion transport from mechanical properties in ideal electrolyte materials
The materials research required to effectively enable alkali metal-based energy storage applications is still in its relative infancy.

important_claims.claim: Single-ion conductors with tethered anions and copolymer approaches can significantly increase the conductivity of alkali metal cations.
important_claims.evidence: This has been demonstrated both experimentally and via computer simulations.

important_claims.claim: Block copolymers used in combination with ionic-liquid electrolytes can provide high ionic conductivity and good mechanical properties.
important_claims.evidence: The block copolymer approach is also demonstrated to simultaneously provide mechanical properties and high ionic conductivity when used in combination with ionic-liquid electrolytes.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The paper provides a comprehensive overview of the current state and future directions in the development of solid-state electrolytes, ionic liquids, and polymers, which can be valuable for researchers looking to explore and improve upon existing electrolyte materials for next-generation batteries.

keynotes.title: Li, Na, K, Mg, Zn, Al, and Ca Anode Interface Chemistries Developed by Solid‐State Electrolytes

keynote.title: Li, Na, K, Mg, Zn, Al, and Ca Anode Interface Chemistries Developed by Solid‐State Electrolytes
keynote.core_problem: Developing solid-state electrolyte (SE) interphases that can meet the practical requirements for reversible anodes in solid-state batteries (SSBs), addressing the challenges associated with metal nucleation, deposition, and stripping, as well as the integration of SEs, anodes, and cathodes.
contributions: Provides a comprehensive review of the fundamentals, structures, thermodynamics, chemistries, and electrochemical kinetics of desirable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) for SSBs.
Discusses theoretical and experimental insights into metal nucleation, deposition, and stripping for reversible cycling of metal anodes.
Highlights recent innovations in anode interface chemistries developed by SEs for various cation carriers, including monovalent and multivalent ions.

gap_signals.text: Interface challenges and strategies for the integration of SEs, anodes, and cathodes, including physical/chemical contacts, space-charge layer, interdiffusion, lattice-mismatch, dendritic growth, chemical reactivity of SEI, current collectors, and thermal instability.

important_claims.claim: Solid-state batteries (SSBs) have high energy density, reversible cycle life, and safe operations compared to commercial Li-ion batteries using flammable liquid electrolytes.
important_claims.evidence: This is a foundational claim based on the general understanding and research in the field of battery technology, which is supported by the review's focus on improving SSBs through SEIs.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The review provides a valuable resource for researchers working on the development of solid-state batteries, offering a detailed overview of the current state of SEI chemistry, the challenges faced, and potential solutions. It serves as a foundation for future research aimed at overcoming the limitations of existing technologies and advancing the performance of SSBs.

keynotes.title: Fast in situ 3D nanoimaging: a new tool for dynamic characterization in materials science

keynote.title: Fast in situ 3D nanoimaging: a new tool for dynamic characterization in materials science
keynote.core_problem: The need for precise and real-time characterization of the nanostructure of advanced materials, especially under realistic conditions such as high temperatures, is not fully met by current X-ray computed tomography (CT) techniques due to limitations in beam and sample stability, spatial resolution, and acquisition time.
contributions: Development of fast X-ray nanotomography capable of imaging at temperatures up to 700°C with nanometer pixel size and acquisition times of a few tens of seconds.

Demonstration of the technique's potential through the observation of early stages of thermally driven phenomena, such as neck curvature evolution in sintering and nucleation of liquid droplets in light alloys.
methods: X-ray computed tomography (CT)
Synchrotron-based fast X-ray nanotomography
experiments_or_evidence: Real-time observations of neck curvature evolution in sintering and nucleation of liquid droplets in light alloys at high temperatures.
limitations: The specific technical details regarding the limitations of the method, such as the exact temperature range, pixel size, and other operational constraints, are not provided in the abstract.

gap_signals.text: The specific technical details regarding the limitations of the method, such as the exact temperature range, pixel size, and other operational constraints, are not provided in the abstract.
The development addresses the gap in achieving both high spatial resolution and fast acquisition times, particularly for in situ 4D imaging at high temperatures.

important_claims.claim: The new method provides unprecedented combination of nanometer pixel size and fast acquisition times, enabling real-time observations of thermal processes in materials.
important_claims.evidence: Observations of neck curvature evolution in sintering and nucleation of liquid droplets in light alloys were successfully performed using the developed technique.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The novel synchrotron-based fast X-ray nanotomography can be applied to study a wide variety of heterogeneous nanoscale dynamics in materials, enhancing the fundamental understanding of physical mechanisms and providing data for model development under realistic conditions.

keynotes.title: Applications of machine learning methods for design and characterization of high-performance fiber-reinforced cementitious composite (HPFRCC): a review

Elaboration on the steps for property prediction and material design considering fresh properties, cracks, and microstructures

keynotes.title: Latest Advancements and Mechanistic Insights into High-Entropy Alloys: Design, Properties and Applications

keynotes.title: Operando Electrochemical Atomic Force Microscopy of Solid–Electrolyte Interphase Formation on Graphite Anodes: The Evolution of SEI Morphology and Mechanical Properties

keynote.title: Operando Electrochemical Atomic Force Microscopy of Solid–Electrolyte Interphase Formation on Graphite Anodes: The Evolution of SEI Morphology and Mechanical Properties
keynote.core_problem: Comprehensive in situ monitoring of the solid–electrolyte interphase (SEI) formation and evolution, along with the measurement of its mechanical properties, is challenging due to the limitations of commonly used characterization techniques.
contributions: Provides new insights into SEI formation during the first lithiation and delithiation of graphite battery anodes using operando electrochemical atomic force microscopy (EC-AFM).
Investigates highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG) as a model system, showing unique morphological and nanomechanical behavior dependent on electrolytes and additives.
Validates findings with real-world battery electrodes by studying individual graphite particles similar to those in commercial systems.

Demonstrates that vinylene carbonate (VC) and fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) are effective additives for enhancing SEI layer stability in 1 M LiPF6/ethylene carbonate/ethyl methyl carbonate (EC/EMC) electrolytes.
methods: Operando electrochemical atomic force microscopy (EC-AFM)
Use of highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG) as a model system
Study of individual graphite particles similar to those in commercial systems
experiments_or_evidence: Observation of SEI formation and evolution during the first lithiation and delithiation of graphite anodes
Comparison of SEI properties with different electrolytes and additives (VC and FEC)

gap_signals.text: Validation of findings with real-world battery electrodes suggests practical relevance and applicability of the results.
The use of operando EC-AFM provides a more comprehensive in situ monitoring of SEI formation and evolution compared to traditional methods.

datasets_or_materials: Individual graphite particles similar to those in commercial systems
1 M LiPF6/ethylene carbonate/ethyl methyl carbonate (EC/EMC) electrolytes with VC and FEC additives

important_claims.claim: Vinylene carbonate (VC) and fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) enhance SEI layer stability in 1 M LiPF6/ethylene carbonate/ethyl methyl carbonate (EC/EMC) electrolytes.
important_claims.evidence: Operando EC-AFM observations show improved structure, density, and mechanical strength of the SEI layer when these additives are used.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This work provides a detailed methodology and results that can be used to further investigate the effects of different electrolytes and additives on SEI formation and properties, which is crucial for improving the performance and lifetime of lithium-ion batteries.

keynotes.title: Recent Progress in Understanding Solid Electrolyte Interphase on Lithium Metal Anodes

keynote.title: Recent Progress in Understanding Solid Electrolyte Interphase on Lithium Metal Anodes
keynote.core_problem: A thorough understanding of the mechanisms of SEI formation and evolution on lithium metal anodes is still inadequate, which affects the performance and cycle life of lithium metal batteries.
contributions: Summarized the compositions, models, and recent progress in characterizing atomic structures of SEI
Elucidated the properties of SEI, including electronic conduction, ionic conduction, stability, and mechanical properties
Discussed the interactions between multiple factors and SEI, and their impact on SEI structures and properties
Reviewed efficient strategies for tailoring SEI with desired properties, both in situ and ex situ

Proposed future directions, including in-operando techniques, multi-modality approaches for characterization of SEI, and AI-assisted understanding of correlations between electrolyte components and SEI properties

keynotes.title: Review on modeling of the anode solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) for lithium-ion batteries

keynote.title: Review on modeling of the anode solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) for lithium-ion batteries
keynote.core_problem: The complex structure of the SEI and the lack of reliable in situ experimental techniques hinder a complete understanding of SEI formation and growth mechanisms.
contributions: Provides an overview of state-of-the-art modeling progress in the investigation of SEI films on the anodes, from electronic structure calculations to mesoscale modeling.
Summarizes and compares multi-scale simulations with each other and with experimental data.
Discusses the computational details of fundamental properties of SEI, including electron tunneling, Li-ion transport, and chemical/mechanical stability.
methods: Electronic structure calculations
Mesoscale modeling
Multi-scale simulations
assumptions: Computational methods can predictively model the fundamentals of SEI.
Integration of computational modeling with experiments can lead to a better understanding of SEI.
limitations: Complete understanding of SEI formation and growth mechanisms is still lacking due to the complex structure of SEI and the lack of reliable in situ experimental techniques.

important_claims.claim: Computational approaches show potential in deconvoluting SEI properties and designing artificial SEI.

keynotes.title: Unveiling the Mystery of LiF within Solid Electrolyte Interphase in Lithium Batteries

keynote.title: Unveiling the Mystery of LiF within Solid Electrolyte Interphase in Lithium Batteries
keynote.core_problem: The specific properties, including structure and composition, of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) in lithium-ion batteries remain largely unclear, particularly regarding the role and function of LiF, a common and important component in SEI.
contributions: Summarizes recent research findings on SEI and focuses on the characteristics of the LiF component.
Consolidates the understanding of SEI formation and the evolution of SEI models.
Discusses the distinctive properties of SEI generated on various negative electrodes and the ongoing scholarly controversy surrounding the function of LiF within SEI.

gap_signals.text: The specific properties, including structure and composition, of the SEI remain largely unclear, particularly the role and function of LiF within SEI.

important_claims.claim: LiF is a common and important component in SEI, but its role and function are subject to debate among researchers.
important_claims.evidence: Numerous studies have focused on SEI, yet there are divergent viewpoints regarding the function of LiF within SEI.

important_claims.claim: Understanding the role of LiF in SEI can contribute to the development of lithium batteries with enhanced electrochemical performance and safety.
important_claims.evidence: The review aims to consolidate the understanding of SEI and the role of the LiF component, which is expected to improve battery technology.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review consolidates existing knowledge and debates about the SEI and LiF, providing a comprehensive foundation for future research aimed at improving the electrochemical performance and safety of lithium batteries.

keynotes.title: Modeling the Solid Electrolyte Interphase: Machine Learning as a Game Changer?

keynote.title: Modeling the Solid Electrolyte Interphase: Machine Learning as a Game Changer?
keynote.core_problem: Understanding the precise formation mechanism, structure, composition, and evolution of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) in batteries, which is crucial for overall battery performance and aging.
contributions: Discussion on how recent advances in data-driven models, especially fast and accurate surrogate simulators and deep generative models, can enhance the understanding of SEI.
Integration of machine learning with physics-based and physics-informed approaches to enable breakthroughs in SEI modeling.
methods: Use of machine learning-enhanced multiscale models
Development of fast and accurate surrogate simulators and deep generative models
assumptions: Machine learning techniques can improve the trade-off between accuracy and accessible time- and length scales in computational modeling of SEI.

keynotes.title: Decoupled Ion Transport via Triadic Molecular Synergy in Flame‐Retardant Quasi‐Solid Electrolytes for Safe Lithium Metal Batteries

keynote.title: Decoupled Ion Transport via Triadic Molecular Synergy in Flame‐Retardant Quasi‐Solid Electrolytes for Safe Lithium Metal Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Traditional design strategies for ionic liquid (IL)‐based quasi‐solid polymer electrolytes (QSPEs) have ignored the interdependencies among components, functions, and interfaces, leading to compromised practical applications with sluggish lithium-ion transport kinetics and safety concerns.
contributions: Proposed a triadic molecular synergy paradigm to decouple lithium-ion conduction mechanisms in flame-retardant QSPEs.

Demonstrated that the designed QSPE achieves a high lithium-ion transference number, forms stable lithium fluoride-dominated interphases, and enhances battery safety through a condensed-phase flame-retardant mechanism.
methods: Used pentaerythritol tetraacrylate-lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI) as the structural framework.
Employed 1-butyl-3-methylimidazole bis (trifluoromethylsulfonyl) imide (BmimTFSI) as a plasticizer to soften the polymer chains and provide an additional ion-transport pathway.
Utilized 2-(perfluorohexyl)ethyl methacrylate (PFMA) to promote LiTFSI dissociation and immobilize TFSI⁻ anions, suppressing cationic competition through strong PFMA−Bmim+ coordination.
experiments_or_evidence: Achieved a high lithium-ion transference number of 0.72.
Formed stable lithium fluoride-dominated interphases.
Enhanced cycling stability in Li symmetric cells, Li||LiFePO4, and Li||LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cells.
assumptions: The interdependencies among components, functions, and interfaces are critical for the performance of QSPEs.
limitations: The paper does not explicitly mention any limitations.

gap_signals.text: The paper does not explicitly mention any limitations.

Traditional design strategy has ignored the interdependencies among 'component-function-interface', leading to compromised practical applications hindered by sluggish lithium-ion transport kinetics and safety concerns.

important_claims.claim: The proposed triadic molecular synergy paradigm significantly enhances the performance and safety of lithium metal batteries.
important_claims.evidence: Achieved a high lithium-ion transference number (0.72), formed stable lithium fluoride-dominated interphases, and enhanced cycling stability in various cell configurations.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: The triadic molecular synergy paradigm can be applied to other types of electrolytes and battery systems to improve ion transport and safety, providing a new approach for the design of high-performance QSPEs.

Discusses fundamental problems and potential solutions related to ion and electron transport behaviors inside the electrode.

Explores structural engineering approaches for designing advanced cathodes, including interlayer intercalation, doping effects, defect engineering, surface coatings, composite formation, and morphology control.
Provides critical research challenges and future perspectives on advanced cathode materials and the development of AZIBs.

keynotes.title: Zinc Ion Transport Kinetics in Zinc‐based Batteries and Its Regulation Strategy

keynote.title: Zinc Ion Transport Kinetics in Zinc‐based Batteries and Its Regulation Strategy
keynote.core_problem: The substantial electrostatic repulsion of Zn ions and the formation of hydrated ionic groups in aqueous electrolytes result in sluggish kinetics for zinc ion insertion into the cathode material, hampering the transport ability and overall electrochemical performance of zinc-ion batteries.
contributions: Systematically summarized recent progress in the regulation strategy of zinc-ion transport kinetics in ZIBs.
Introduced mechanisms for zinc ion transport including Zn2+ insertion/extraction, H+ or H2O/Zn2+ co-insertion/extraction, conversion reaction, and coordination reaction.
Summarized cathode material design strategies for fast zinc-ion transport kinetics, such as soft lattice construction, doping effects, defect introduction, morphology control, and interface design.
Provided future research directions like high-entropy design, multi-scale simulation, and machine learning to develop high-performance zinc-ion batteries at ultralow operation temperatures.
methods: Review and analysis of existing literature on zinc-ion battery mechanisms and cathode material designs.

gap_signals.text: High-entropy design, multi-scale simulation, and machine learning are suggested as areas for future research to enhance the performance of zinc-ion batteries at ultralow operation temperatures.

important_claims.claim: Soft lattice construction, doping effects, defect introduction, morphology control, and interface design can improve the zinc-ion transport kinetics in cathode materials.
important_claims.evidence: These strategies have been systematically reviewed and shown to enhance the performance of ZIBs by addressing the kinetic issues related to zinc ion transport.

important_claims.claim: Mechanisms such as Zn2+ insertion/extraction, H+ or H2O/Zn2+ co-insertion/extraction, conversion reaction, and coordination reaction play a crucial role in the transport of zinc ions in ZIBs.
important_claims.evidence: These mechanisms have been identified and discussed in the context of their impact on the electrochemical performance of ZIBs.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: This review provides a comprehensive overview of the current state of zinc-ion battery technology, highlighting key strategies and mechanisms that can be further explored and developed. It also suggests future research directions, which can guide new studies and innovations in the field.

Discusses full battery performance, degradation mechanisms, in situ/ex situ characterization, and theoretical calculations.
